ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for 'cocktail drink (any flavour)'; mapped to broader class FOODON_03301740 (alcoholic beverage) as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for generic cocktail drink; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of a cocktail drink is approximately 200 ml (200 g), equivalent to one standard cocktail glass as commonly served in bars and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Cocktail drink (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Cocktail drink (any flavour)', cr_id D01030001. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; set to 0.0 as consistent with 0.0 g total fat. Detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrients, and water content not reported by HPB; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a generic cocktail drink. The carbohydrate content (11.6 g/100 g) derives primarily from fruit juice, syrups, and mixers. A GI of 65 is estimated by analogy with fruit juice and sugar-sweetened mixer beverages as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 23.2 g per 200 g serving) / 100 ≈ 15.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content raises blood pressure and interacts with antihypertensive medications
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid alcoholic cocktails entirely, as alcohol directly elevates blood pressure and antagonises the effect of antihypertensive medications regardless of the low sodium content (2 mg/100 g) of the beverage.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation; carbohydrate from mixers adds glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid alcoholic cocktails because alcohol impairs hepatic glucose output and can precipitate hypoglycaemia in those on insulin or sulfonylureas, while the 11.6 g/100 g carbohydrate from fruit juices and syrups further elevates postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol consumption increases cardiovascular risk including arrhythmia, cardiomyopathy, and hypertriglyceridaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid alcoholic cocktails, as regular alcohol intake is associated with elevated triglycerides, atrial fibrillation risk, and cardiomyopathy; no safe threshold of alcohol consumption has been established for those with established CVD.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol is pro-inflammatory and interacts dangerously with RA disease-modifying drugs including methotrexate and biologics
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis must avoid alcoholic cocktails entirely, as alcohol is directly pro-inflammatory and carries serious hepatotoxicity risk when combined with methotrexate or biologic therapies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A cocktail drink (any flavour) is a generic category of mixed alcoholic beverage combining distilled spirits — such as vodka, rum, gin, tequila, or whisky — with fruit juices, flavoured syrups, sodas, or other non-alcoholic mixers. The resulting drink varies widely in flavour, colour, and alcohol content depending on the specific recipe and proportions used. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore Foodo database records 46 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, and 11.6 g carbohydrate, with the carbohydrate derived entirely from the mixer components (juice, syrup, or soda) rather than from the alcohol itself. Sodium is negligible at 2 mg/100 g. Protein and fat are naturally zero in this beverage category, as distilled spirits and standard mixers contribute no meaningful protein or fat. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium) by analogy with fruit juice and sugar-sweetened mixer beverages. Clinically, cocktail drinks are contraindicated for individuals with type 2 diabetes (alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation and mixers add glycaemic load), hypertension (alcohol directly raises blood pressure), cardiovascular disease (alcohol elevates triglycerides and arrhythmia risk), and rheumatoid arthritis (alcohol interacts with methotrexate and biologics). This record represents a generic composite entry covering any flavour variant of a standard cocktail drink as classified in the Singapore HPB food composition system.
common_user_queries: how many calories in a cocktail drink
is cocktail drink suitable for diabetics
can I drink cocktails if I have high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D01030001, confidence 0.95). Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the only approved GI source; this does not reduce overall confidence given the transparent documentation.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: distilled spirits and standard cocktail mixers (juice, soda, syrup) contain no meaningful protein. fat_g is naturally zero: this beverage category contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB despite 11.6 g/100 g total carbohydrate; this likely reflects a reporting convention where total carbohydrate includes alcohol-derived carbohydrate equivalents or the sugar assay method used; value preserved as reported. saturated_fat_g set to 0.0 consistent with 0.0 g total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this generic cocktail drink category are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. calcium_mg and iron_mg set to 0.0 as explicitly reported by HPB. dietary_flags: is_halal set to false as this is an alcoholic beverage; is_gluten_free set to false as grain-based spirits (whisky, some vodkas) may contain gluten. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on fruit juice and sugar-sweetened mixer beverages per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for a generic cocktail drink category.
